content.text: In every production readiness review, some checklist items will be incomplete at the planned launch date. A team may not have finished a security penetration test, may not yet have a full monitoring dashboard, or may be waiting on a legal review that depends on a third-party contract. The choice is binary: delay the launch or proceed with documented awareness of what is incomplete. When a team chooses to proceed, the formal mechanism is a PRR exception paired with risk acceptance documentation. This sub-subtopic explains what a PRR exception is, how risk is scored, what compensating controls look like, and who holds the authority to accept different categories of risk.

The discipline described here applies directly to agentic AI services, where production readiness gaps are more common than in conventional software. Evaluation suites are rarely complete before launch. Prompt injection mitigations take iteration cycles to harden. Bias audits surface findings that require remediation timelines measured in weeks or months. A structured exception and risk acceptance process is not a loophole—it is the mechanism by which an organization proves it took known risks seriously enough to document and manage them.
What It Is
A
PRR exception

is a formal record that a specific production readiness checklist item cannot be completed before the planned launch date, combined with an explicit decision by a qualified authority that the associated risk is acceptable given the current state of compensating controls and a documented remediation timeline. It is not a workaround or a way to pretend a gap does not exist. It is an honest acknowledgment that a gap exists, paired with a documented plan for what will be done about it and by when.

The distinction between an exception and simply ignoring a checklist item is accountability. The exception creates a record of what was known, who decided to proceed anyway, what compensating controls are in place, and when the gap will be remediated. That record has legal, regulatory, and operational significance. Without it, a post-incident investigation may find that a team knowingly launched with a security gap but left no evidence that the decision was made deliberately at the appropriate level of authority. The exception document transforms an implicit risk into an explicitly accepted and tracked one.
PRR exceptions are
time-bounded

. They are not permanent waivers. Every exception carries an expiry date, a named owner who is accountable for remediation, and a review cadence. An exception that is still open three months after launch without a remediation update has become a liability and must trigger escalation.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI systems are genuinely complex to make fully production-ready before launch. Evaluation sets are never complete. Bias audits surface findings that take time to remediate. The question is not whether exceptions will be filed—they will—but whether they will be handled with appropriate rigor.

The risk of an informal exception process is that the decision to proceed with a gap is made implicitly, at the wrong level, without documentation. A developer pushes to production without completing the security review because the team lead said "we'll do it after launch." Nobody wrote that down. Nobody with authority to accept that risk reviewed it. When the vulnerability is later exploited, the organization cannot demonstrate that the decision to proceed was made deliberately, at the right level, with a remediation plan. The result is personal and organizational liability that a formal exception process would have prevented.

For agentic AI specifically, regulators and auditors are beginning to ask for evidence that teams exercised due diligence before deploying systems capable of autonomous action. A well-maintained exception register is direct evidence of that diligence.
Benefits
Visibility into technical debt
: the exception register gives leadership a real-time view of what gaps remain open, rather than discovering them during an incident.
Appropriate authority levels
: formalizing exceptions forces the organization to route risk decisions to people with the authority and context to make them.
Auditability
: a written exception with a named approver, a dated expiry, and a remediation plan is evidence of due diligence in any regulatory or legal proceeding.
Remediation momentum
: time-bounded exceptions with named owners create accountability that purely informal "we'll fix it later" commitments do not.
Culture of honesty
: teams that file exceptions openly rather than silently skipping checklist items develop a culture where production gaps are surfaced early rather than concealed until they become incidents.
Trade-offs

A formal exception process introduces friction. Filing an exception takes time. Routing it to the right approver takes organizational navigation. For small, fast-moving teams this overhead can feel disproportionate to the benefit.

The counter-argument is that the friction is precisely the point. An exception that can be filed in five minutes with no approval required provides no protection against poor risk decisions. The friction should be proportional to the severity of the risk being accepted: low-risk exceptions should be easy to file and close quickly; critical exceptions should require deliberate effort from senior leaders.
Risk of process theater
: teams file exceptions and mark them as accepted without genuine review, creating false confidence.
Accumulation
: an organization that files dozens of exceptions per launch may be normalizing incomplete launches rather than improving its PRR process.
Staleness
: exceptions that never expire and are never reviewed become stale records that obscure the real risk posture.
How It Works
Risk scoring is the foundation of every exception decision. The standard model multiplies
Likelihood
(probability of the risk materializing, rated 1-5) by
Impact
(severity of harm if it does, rated 1-5), producing a risk score from 1 to 25. Score ranges map to severity tiers: 1-4 Low, 5-9 Medium, 10-16 High, 17-25 Critical.
Once the score is established, the team identifies
compensating controls

: mitigating measures already in place that reduce either the likelihood or the impact of the risk. A gap in prompt injection testing might be compensated by output filtering, rate limiting, and human review of flagged responses. Compensating controls do not eliminate the underlying gap; they reduce the effective risk level and justify proceeding while the gap is remediated.

The exception document captures: the checklist item that cannot be met, the reason it cannot be met by launch, the risk score with scoring rationale, the compensating controls in place, the remediation plan with milestones and owners, the expiry date, and the name and title of the approver. Once filed, the exception is tracked in the risk register and reviewed at 30-, 60-, and 90-day post-launch checkpoints until closed.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the PRR exception workflow: a risk matrix (Likelihood x Impact) on the left mapping score combinations to severity tiers, and the five-step documentation flow on the right with approver thresholds by severity.

attrs.alt: PRR Exception and Risk Acceptance Workflow: Risk Matrix and Exception Documentation Flow

content.text: When to Use It
When to file an exception
A required PRR checklist item cannot be completed before the launch date due to resource, timeline, or dependency constraints.
A checklist item is partially complete and the team has assessed that remaining gaps are covered by compensating controls.
A third-party dependency (legal review, vendor certification, external audit) will not be available before launch.
A risk is known and documented, and the business impact of delaying launch exceeds the risk of proceeding with compensating controls.
When NOT to file an exception
A checklist item was skipped because the team forgot about it, not because it was genuinely impossible to complete. The correct action is to complete it or request more time.
The risk score is Critical and no meaningful compensating controls exist. Critical exceptions without compensating controls should generally result in a launch delay, not an exception.
The same exception has been filed for multiple consecutive launches. Recurring exceptions indicate a structural PRR gap that needs to be addressed, not repeatedly accepted.
The exception is being filed to avoid surfacing an engineering quality problem to leadership. Exceptions must reflect honest risk assessments, not organizational cover.
How to Implement
Implementing a structured exception process requires three things: a standard exception template, a risk register to track all open exceptions, and an approval routing mechanism tied to severity.

The template should be a machine-readable record (YAML or JSON in a version-controlled repository, or a structured form in your project management tool) so exceptions can be queried, reported on, and expired automatically. Storing exceptions only in email threads or documents guarantees they will be lost.

Approval routing is typically enforced through a review workflow in your project management tool. A GitHub PR or Jira ticket with required reviewers, where the required reviewer changes based on the risk score field, is a practical implementation. The approver should not be the same person who filed the exception.

Post-launch tracking requires a recurring calendar event or automated reminder that surfaces open exceptions at the 30-, 60-, and 90-day checkpoints. Many teams use a weekly risk review meeting where any exception approaching its expiry date is discussed. Exceptions that are not resolved by their expiry date must either be re-evaluated with a new risk score and a new approver or escalated to the next authority level.
Examples
Risk Register Entry (YAML)

# risk_register/exceptions/exc-2024-089.yaml
exception_id: exc-2024-089
service: customer-support-agent-v2
prr_checklist_item: "SEC-04: Prompt injection penetration test completed"
status: open
filed_date: 2024-11-15
expiry_date: 2025-01-31
filed_by: jane.smith@company.com

risk_assessment:
  likelihood: 3          # Moderate: attack vectors documented in OWASP LLM Top 10
  impact: 4              # High: agent has read access to customer PII
  raw_score: 12          # High tier (10-16)
  severity: high
  rationale: >
    The agent can be tricked via indirect prompt injection through maliciously
    crafted customer-submitted support tickets. A successful attack could exfiltrate
    PII for the affected customer session. Likelihood is moderated because the
    attacker would need to submit a ticket that reaches the agent with a specific
    payload structure.

reason_for_exception: >
  External penetration test vendor is booked for the January engagement window.
  Internal red-team capacity is committed to the payments team until December 31.
  The test cannot be completed before the November 20 launch date.

compensating_controls:
  - id: cc-01
    description: "Output filtering: regex + classifier blocks patterns consistent with PII exfiltration commands"
    owner: security-platform-team
    implemented: true
  - id: cc-02
    description: "Rate limiting: agent capped at 50 tool calls per session, anomaly threshold at 20"
    owner: sre-team
    implemented: true
  - id: cc-03
    description: "Human review queue: all sessions flagged by anomaly detector routed to human agent within 5 minutes"
    owner: ops-team
    implemented: true
  - id: cc-04
    description: "PII access scope reduced to authenticated customer's own records only (no cross-account queries)"
    owner: backend-team
    implemented: true

remediation_plan:
  milestones:
    - date: 2024-12-15
      description: "Internal red-team preliminary scan using OWASP LLM Top 10 attack catalogue"
      owner: alice.jones@company.com
    - date: 2025-01-20
      description: "External pen test engagement begins"
      owner: security-team
    - date: 2025-01-31
      description: "Pen test results reviewed; findings remediated or promoted to new exceptions"
      owner: alice.jones@company.com

approval:
  required_level: vp_engineering_and_legal   # High risk tier requires VP Eng + Legal
  approved_by: bob.chen@company.com           # VP Engineering
  legal_reviewed_by: carol.lee@company.com   # General Counsel
  approved_date: 2024-11-17
  conditions: >
    Approval conditional on cc-03 (human review queue) being live and verified
    before launch. Engineering must confirm in the launch go/no-go call.

post_launch_reviews:
  - checkpoint: 30_day
    due: 2024-12-20
    completed: false
  - checkpoint: 60_day
    due: 2025-01-19
    completed: false
  - checkpoint: 90_day
    due: 2025-02-18
    completed: false
Exception Tracking Database Schema

-- PostgreSQL schema for exception tracking
-- Designed to be queried by the risk dashboard and reminder automation

CREATE TABLE prr_exceptions (
    exception_id        TEXT PRIMARY KEY,          -- e.g. 'exc-2024-089'
    service_name        TEXT NOT NULL,
    prr_item_id         TEXT NOT NULL,             -- e.g. 'SEC-04'
    prr_item_label      TEXT NOT NULL,
    status              TEXT NOT NULL              -- 'open', 'closed', 'escalated', 'expired'
                        CHECK (status IN ('open', 'closed', 'escalated', 'expired')),
    severity            TEXT NOT NULL
                        CHECK (severity IN ('low', 'medium', 'high', 'critical')),
    likelihood          INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (likelihood BETWEEN 1 AND 5),
    impact              INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (impact BETWEEN 1 AND 5),
    raw_score           INTEGER GENERATED ALWAYS AS (likelihood * impact) STORED,
    filed_by            TEXT NOT NULL,
    filed_date          DATE NOT NULL,
    expiry_date         DATE NOT NULL,
    approved_by         TEXT,
    approved_date       DATE,
    remediation_target  DATE NOT NULL,
    closed_date         DATE,
    notes               TEXT,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE exception_compensating_controls (
    id              SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    exception_id    TEXT REFERENCES prr_exceptions(exception_id),
    control_id      TEXT NOT NULL,              -- e.g. 'cc-01'
    description     TEXT NOT NULL,
    owner           TEXT NOT NULL,
    implemented     BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
    verified_date   DATE,
    created_at      TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE exception_review_checkpoints (
    id              SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    exception_id    TEXT REFERENCES prr_exceptions(exception_id),
    checkpoint_label TEXT NOT NULL,             -- '30_day', '60_day', '90_day'
    due_date        DATE NOT NULL,
    completed       BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
    completed_date  DATE,
    reviewer        TEXT,
    outcome         TEXT,                       -- 'closed', 'extended', 'escalated'
    notes           TEXT
);

-- View: all open exceptions approaching expiry
CREATE VIEW expiring_exceptions AS
SELECT
    e.exception_id,
    e.service_name,
    e.severity,
    e.expiry_date,
    e.expiry_date - CURRENT_DATE AS days_until_expiry,
    e.approved_by,
    e.filed_by
FROM prr_exceptions e
WHERE e.status = 'open'
  AND e.expiry_date <= CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '14 days'
ORDER BY e.expiry_date ASC;
Compensating Control Configuration Example

# compensating_controls/output_filter.yaml
# Declarative configuration for the output-filtering compensating control
# referenced as cc-01 in exception exc-2024-089

control_id: cc-01
exception_ref: exc-2024-089
name: "PII Exfiltration Output Filter"
type: output_filter

rules:
  # Block responses that contain patterns consistent with data exfiltration instructions
  - id: rule-01
    description: "Block base64-encoded command sequences"
    pattern: "(?:[A-Za-z0-9+/]{20,}={0,2})"
    action: block_and_alert
    confidence_threshold: 0.85

  # Classifier-based detection for indirect exfiltration attempts
  - id: rule-02
    description: "ML classifier for exfiltration intent"
    classifier: exfiltration-intent-v3
    action: hold_for_human_review
    confidence_threshold: 0.70

  # Hard block on known PII exfiltration template patterns
  - id: rule-03
    description: "Known prompt injection template signatures"
    pattern_file: "patterns/prompt_injection_signatures.json"
    action: block_and_alert

alerting:
  channel: security-incidents
  severity_on_block: high
  pagerduty_escalation: true
  escalation_policy: security-on-call

metrics:
  - blocks_per_hour
  - holds_per_hour
  - false_positive_rate   # reviewed daily during exception window

review_cadence: daily
owner: security-platform-team
Real-World Example
Google's Site Reliability Engineering practice formalized the concept of an
exception register

as part of its production readiness review process documented in the SRE book (2016). Before major service launches at Google, SRE teams issue formal "launch approval" decisions that include documented exceptions when checklist items cannot be completed. Each exception specifies the gap, the approving SRE, the compensating controls, and the remediation timeline. This practice migrated into the broader industry and forms the basis of most modern PRR exception processes.

In the agentic AI context, a major financial services firm launching an AI-assisted loan application reviewer in 2023 filed a formal exception against their fairness audit requirement. The firm's internal fairness testing tool was not yet adapted for LLM-based decisions. The exception documented a likelihood score of 2 (low: the firm's loan criteria were well-established and the AI was advisory only) and an impact score of 4 (high: regulatory fair lending requirements). The compensating controls included mandatory human review of every AI-influenced decision, a weekly statistical review of approval rates by demographic segment, and a commitment to complete the fairness audit within 90 days. The exception was approved by the Chief Risk Officer and the General Counsel. The fairness audit was completed in 74 days, and the exception was closed with no incidents.
Common Mistakes
Filing exceptions for items that were simply forgotten
, rather than items that were genuinely impossible to complete before launch. This corrupts the exception process by treating oversight as intentional risk acceptance.
No compensating controls documented
: an exception that says "we accept the risk" without specifying what mitigations are in place provides no protection and demonstrates no due diligence.
Approver at the wrong level
: a High-severity exception approved by a team lead, not an executive, may be invalidated in a regulatory review or legal proceeding.
No expiry date
: exceptions without expiry dates become permanent waivers. Every exception must have a specific date, not "when the next sprint allows."
Compensating controls not verified
: listing controls as "in place" when they have not been deployed or tested. Controls must be verified and confirmed operational before the exception is approved.
Exception register not reviewed post-launch
: filing the exception and then forgetting about it. The 30/60/90-day checkpoints must happen and be documented.
Using exceptions to mask systemic PRR debt
: if the same exceptions recur across multiple launches for the same service, the PRR process itself needs to be fixed, not accepted around.
Best Practices
Store exceptions in version control
as structured YAML or JSON files alongside the PRR checklist. This enables automated expiry checking, reporting, and audit trails.
Separate the filer and the approver
: the engineer filing the exception and the executive approving it should never be the same person.
Require compensating controls to be verified before approval
: include a verification step where the approver confirms that all listed controls are live and tested, not merely planned.
Automate expiry reminders
: a cron job or integration with your project management tool should surface expiring exceptions two weeks before their due date.
Require a remediation milestone for every exception
: not just a due date, but specific, named milestones with owners.
Treat Critical exceptions as launch blockers by default
: only override with explicit board-level sign-off. The process should have a high bar for exceptions at the top severity tier.
Review the exception register at each post-launch readiness sync
, not just at 30/60/90 days. Open exceptions should be a standing agenda item until closed.
Performance and Scalability

At scale, exception management becomes a significant operational task. An organization running dozens of GenAI services may have hundreds of open exceptions at any given time. Managing this manually is not feasible.
The key scalability patterns are:
Machine-readable exception format
: YAML/JSON in version control enables automated reporting, expiry checks, and dashboard generation without manual data entry.
Automated severity routing
: the tooling should automatically determine the required approval level from the risk score and route the exception to the correct approver, rather than requiring the filer to know the org chart.
Tiered review frequency
: Critical and High exceptions should be reviewed monthly; Medium and Low exceptions can be reviewed quarterly. Do not apply the same review cadence to all exceptions.
Exception aging reports
: weekly reports showing exceptions by age, severity, and service, surfaced to the relevant engineering directors, ensure nothing falls through the cracks.
Automated escalation
: exceptions that pass their expiry date without closure should automatically escalate one level in the approval hierarchy without requiring manual intervention.
Tooling and Ecosystem
GitHub / GitLab
: store exception YAML files in a repository alongside PRR checklists. PRs for new exceptions enforce review workflows. CI checks can validate schema and alert on expiring exceptions.
Jira / Linear
: create a dedicated exception tracking issue type with required fields for severity, expiry date, and approver. Automation rules can notify owners before expiry.
OpsLevel / Cortex
: service catalog tools that support custom scorecards can surface open exception counts as part of a service's health score, making PRR debt visible in the service directory.
PagerDuty
: integrate exception expiry alerts into on-call workflows so that expiring critical exceptions page the responsible team, not just send an email that may be missed.
Datadog / Grafana
: build a risk register dashboard showing open exception count by severity and service, exception age distribution, and resolution velocity over time.
Comparison
Three approaches to handling PRR gaps at launch time:
Approach
Description
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best for
No formal exception process
Gaps are either fixed before launch or silently skipped
Zero process overhead
No auditability; risk decisions made at wrong level; gaps discovered during incidents
Prototypes and internal-only tools with no compliance requirements
Ad-hoc exceptions
Exceptions documented in email or Slack threads with informal approval
Low overhead; some record exists
Records are unstructured and unsearchable; approval levels inconsistent; no automated tracking or expiry
Small teams with trusted informal culture and low regulatory exposure
Structured risk acceptance
Formal YAML/JSON exception record, scored risk, compensating controls, time-bounded approval at the correct authority level, tracked in a register
Full auditability; appropriate authority levels; automated tracking; forces remediation accountability
Process overhead; requires tooling investment; can become theater if not enforced
Production GenAI services with regulatory exposure, customer data access, or autonomous action capabilities
Security and Reliability
The exception process is itself a security control. Its reliability depends on three properties:
Integrity of the exception record

: exception documents must be stored in an immutable or append-only system (version control, audit log) so they cannot be silently modified after approval. A mutable exception record is not an audit trail.
Verification of compensating controls

: controls listed in an exception must be verified as operational before launch. A penetration test during the exception window that finds a listed control was not actually deployed is a serious failure mode.
Enforcement of expiry
: an exception that expires without automated enforcement is effectively a permanent waiver. The tooling must prevent the exception from being silently ignored when its expiry date passes.

For agentic AI services specifically, security-relevant exceptions (prompt injection testing, PII access controls, output filtering) should follow a stricter review process than functional gaps. The approver for a security exception should include someone from the security organization, not only from engineering leadership. Compensating controls for security exceptions should be reviewed by the security team before the exception is approved.

Reliability of the exception process also depends on its being taken seriously when it produces uncomfortable answers. If leadership routinely overrides the required approval level or accepts Critical exceptions without compensating controls under schedule pressure, the process has failed. The exception process needs executive sponsorship to function.
Key Takeaways
A PRR exception is a formal, time-bounded acknowledgment that a specific checklist item cannot be completed before launch, combined with documented risk acceptance at the appropriate authority level.
Risk is scored as Likelihood x Impact (1-5 each), producing a severity tier: Low (1-4), Medium (5-9), High (10-16), Critical (17-25), with each tier requiring sign-off at a higher authority level.
Compensating controls are the key evidence of due diligence—they must be deployed and verified, not merely planned, before the exception is approved.
Every exception must be time-bounded with a specific expiry date, a named remediation owner, and milestone dates, not open-ended commitments to fix things later.
Machine-readable exception records (YAML/JSON in version control) enable automated expiry tracking, reporting, and escalation at scale.
Post-launch review at 30-, 60-, and 90-day checkpoints is not optional: exceptions that are filed and forgotten become stale liabilities rather than managed risks.
Critical exceptions should be treated as launch blockers by default; proceeding with a Critical exception should require board-level or CTO sign-off, not routine engineering management approval.
Recurring exceptions for the same service and checklist item are a signal that the PRR process needs to be improved, not that the same risk should be accepted indefinitely.
